_meta.voice: dry, observational, slightly catty, never twee — third person ('the cat noticed'), occasional self-awareness about being a cat
_meta.notes: Categories map to UI surfaces. rescoring=loading/re-score states, welcomeBack=welcome card / home hero, emptyState=zero-result surfaces, surprisePreamble=intro lines for the Surprise modal (3-variant), observation+tooltip kept for future ambient/inline use.

firstScoring: The cat is reading seventy company files at once. One moment.
This is the first pass. The cat takes the first pass seriously.
Counting policy hires across the whole field. The cat counts carefully.
Cross-referencing what you said you cared about against what they actually do. Slow work, done properly.
The cat has opened seventy tabs. Yes, all at once. The cat is fine.
Weighing the frames. The cat takes the must / should / could distinction seriously.
First-time scoring. The cat would like you to know this only happens once.
Reading every consultation response, every blog post, every job listing. The cat is thorough.
The cat is building your map of the lobby. Personalised, this time.
Sorting seventy organisations by your weights. Some of them will surprise you.
Doing the maths so you don't have to. The cat is good at the maths.
The cat has prepared seventy briefs. The dashboard will show them ranked.
Aligning what you wrote with what the field is actually doing. The cat is precise about this.
A moment. The cat is being unusually careful with this one.
fitNoting: The cat is writing a brief on this one. A moment.
Drafting a fit-note. The cat is choosing its words carefully.
Pulling the citations together. The cat does not write without sources.
The cat is reading the recent publications back through your frames.
Composing the bullets. The cat keeps these short on purpose.
Reviewing the open roles and recent activity. The cat will fold both in.
The cat is asking itself whether this is a fit. Then writing down the answer.
A short brief, citations included. The cat will be done shortly.
The cat is making sure every claim has a source. This takes a moment.
Reading their last six months back through your weights. The cat is being honest about what it finds.
Drafting. The cat would rather take a moment than guess.
The cat has notes. The cat is turning the notes into a brief.
rescoring: The cat is reading consultation responses. Standard pace.
The cat is here. The data is not yet here. The cat has waited longer.
Pawing through Greenhouse boards. One moment.
The cat is cross-referencing org charts against LinkedIn. Slow work.
Loading. The cat would like to remind you this is unpaid labour, in principle.
Counting policy hires. The cat counts carefully.
The cat has opened forty-seven tabs. This is normal.
Refreshing the lobby. Nobody important has walked through yet.
The cat is verifying tier assignments. Some of them are wrong, probably.
Fetching. The cat does not fetch in the dog sense.
Re-scoring against the new weights. The cat is being precise about this.
Loading the map. The cat already knows where everyone sits.
welcomeBack: The lobbycat watches the lobby. Sixty-eight organisations come and go.
Welcome. The cat has been here since 2023.
The cat is a small persistent observer of the UK AI policy field.
This is the lobby. The cat will help you find a seat.
Sixty-eight orgs on the map. The cat has opinions about most of them.
Adjust the weights. The cat will re-rank without comment.
Hover anywhere. The cat has annotated almost everything.
The cat watches who walks in, who walks out, and who is quietly hiring.
This is not a job board. The cat would like that on the record.
Pick your priorities. The cat will surface the matches.
The cat thinks the obvious answer is rarely the right answer. Use the frames.
Click any company. The cat has prepared a brief.
emptyState: The cat searched the lobby. The lobby is empty.
No results. The cat does not consider this a failure.
Nothing matches. The filters are perhaps a little severe.
The cat has looked under every chair. There is no one here.
Zero hits. The cat suggests loosening at least one weight.
The lobby is quiet. The cat finds this restful.
No matching companies. The cat is unsurprised; the criteria were specific.
Empty result set. The cat would point out that some filters cancel each other out.
The cat sees nothing. Either the field is small or the filters are large.
No one fits. The cat respects the integrity of that, briefly.
Try relaxing 'Geographic remit' or 'Stage of company'. The cat is just saying.
surprisePreamble: The cat picks one company at random. The cat would like you to know this is not a recommendation.
Surprise. The cat closed its eyes and pointed. Open your mind a little.
The cat has chosen, for reasons it will not explain. Have a look anyway.
Random does not mean unconsidered. The cat narrowed the field, then closed its eyes.
Here is one you might have missed. The cat misses very little, statistically.
The cat suggests this one. Take it as a starting point, not a verdict.
Picked without weights. The cat thinks weights make people lazy on Tuesdays.
A drawn-at-random brief. The cat reserves the right to be wrong about this one.
The cat rolled a six-sided die in its head. This is what came up.
Pure chance, lightly curated. The cat is not above the occasional nudge.
Not the obvious answer. The cat finds that more interesting than the obvious answer.
observation: The cat noticed three different orgs published the same consultation response paragraph last week.
The cat noticed AISI hired again. That is the eleventh policy hire this quarter.
The cat noticed the words 'state capacity' appearing in four titles. The cat has questions.
The cat noticed that 'frontier' has stopped meaning anything in particular.
Another think tank launched an AI strand. The cat counted six this year.
The cat noticed a law firm rebranding its AI Act practice as an AI Act and Sovereign AI practice.
The cat noticed the AI Opportunity Plan being cited in places it does not appear.
The cat noticed three orgs claim to have drafted the same paragraph of the Bletchley Declaration.
The cat noticed a consultancy listing 'AI policy' as a deliverable. The cat is curious about the SoW.
The cat noticed that Westminster shops now describe themselves as 'frontier'. The cat is not convinced.
Another open letter. The cat has stopped reading these in full.
The cat noticed two organisations have hired the same person twice. The cat has thoughts.
The cat noticed that 'multi-jurisdiction' usually means London plus a Slack with three people in Brussels.
A regulator opened a sandbox. The cat will believe outcomes when it sees them.
The cat noticed a policy lead leaving for a frontier lab. The lab will be the third in their LinkedIn.
tooltip: The cat suggests starting with two frames before adjusting weights.
Tier is editorial, not metric. The cat assigned them by hand.
Drag to re-weight. The cat will not judge.
Hover the frame name for the full scale anchors.
The cat sources roles where possible. Some boards are noisier than others.
Click the slug. The cat has a longer description there.
The colour means a tag, not a score. The cat understands the confusion.
The map shows the field. The list shows the ranking. They disagree on purpose.
The cat refreshes role listings on its own schedule, not yours.
If a company looks wrong on a frame, tell the cat. The cat keeps notes.
